statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
This is the easy version of the problem. The difference between the versions is that in this version, you must compute a value for one sequence. You can hack only if you solved all versions of this problem.

Let's define an algorithm to generate a sequence of $ m+k $ integers as follows:

1. First, receive a sequence $ x $ of $ m $ integers as input. If $ k=0 $ , terminate immediately and return the sequence $ x $ .
2. Then, select any index $ 1 \le i \le |x| $ and insert $ (x_i+1) $ immediately after the element $ x_i $ .
3. If $ x $ contains exactly $ m+k $ integers, terminate and return the sequence $ x $ . Otherwise, return to the second step.

Alice knows that this algorithm was used by an ancient civilization in order to hide their secrets safely. Alice wants to learn the knowledge that they wanted to hide, but it is not an easy job to infer the input from the output of the algorithm.

Given a sequence $ a $ of $ n $ integers, determine the length of the shortest sequence that could be given as an input for the algorithm to generate $ a $ .

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $ n $ ( $ 1 \le n \le 300\,000 $ ).

The second line of each test case contains $ n $ integers $ a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n $ ( $ 1 \le a_i \le 10^9 $ ).

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 300\,000 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output the length of the shortest sequence on a separate line.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
5
5
1 2 3 4 5
5
1 3 5 7 9
5
1 2 5 6 5
7
1 2 4 5 3 7 8
9
9 8 9 2 3 4 4 5 3
Output
1
5
3
4
3

Note
In the first test case, the sequence $ [1] $ can generate the sequence $ a=[1,2,3,4,5] $ by the following process:

$$$
[1] \to [1,\color{red}{2}] \to [1,2,\color{red}{3}] \to [1,2,3,\color{red}{4}] \to [1,2,3,4,\color{red}{5}]
$$$

In the second test case, the only sequence that can generate $ a=[1,3,5,7,9] $ is $ a $ itself.

solutions.title: Editorial
solutions.content:
2201A1 - Lost Civilization (Easy Version)
There are two solutions to the easy version. They both utilize one observation:
Each element of the initial sequence corresponds to a subsegment of the returned sequence.
The two solutions are as follows:
Solution 1: Process in the order of
left to right
.
Let's check for each element if it must be a new subsegment or should be merged into the previous subsegment. Let's say the first element of the previous subsegment was $$$x$$$, and the last element was $$$y$$$. We definitely know that $$$x \le y$$$. Let's think of it in a case-by-case analysis:
If $$$a_i \le x$$$, then nothing in the previous subsegment could add $$$a_i$$$. This must be a new subsegment.
If $$$a_i = y+1$$$, then you can construct $$$a_i$$$ from the last element. This can be merged into the previous subsegment.
If $$$x+1 \le a_i \le y$$$, then we can perform until $$$a_i-1$$$ is found in the previous subsegment, add $$$a_i$$$, then perform the rest of the operations. This can be merged into the previous subsegment.
If $$$y+1  \lt  a_i$$$, then nothing could add $$$a_i$$$. Note that the maximum value of the previous subsegment
does not matter
. This is because adding $$$a_i$$$ and then inserting smaller values right before it is impossible.
Therefore, $$$a_i$$$ can be merged into the previous subsegment if and only if $$$x+1 \le a_i \le y+1$$$.
Greedily merging according to this rule yields the optimal initial sequence in $$$\mathcal{O}(n)$$$ time.
Solution 2: Process in the order of
right to left
.
Let's maintain a stack of elements. For each earlier element $$$a_i$$$ we see, we will eliminate all occurrences of $$$a_i+1$$$ on top of the stack. Afterwards, we push $$$a_i$$$ to the top of the stack.
This can be seen as performing the operations in reverse order, and thus this algorithm only gets sequences that can construct the given sequence.
The stack after this algorithm finishes (from top to bottom) gives you the optimal initial sequence. The algorithm has time complexity $$$\mathcal{O}(n)$$$.
